perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Information” shall, mean all intellectual property, business plans, financial records and strategies, marketing plans, contacts, trade secrets, information, materials, documentation and software, as well as copies of all such materials made thereof as authorized by this Agreement, including written, oral, or verbal disclosures.
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Information” shall mean any information disclosed under this agreement, regardless of whether it is marked as confidential or proprietary and includes intellectual property, business plans, financial records and strategies, marketing plans, contacts, trade secrets, information, materials, documentation and software, as well as copies of all such materials, including written, oral, or verbal disclosures.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement for information to be explicitly designated as confidential, the definition is broadened. This creates ambiguity and potential conflict with trade secret laws, which often require reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: DUTSA defines trade secrets as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known and is subject to efforts to maintain its secrecy. By defining 'Confidential Information' to include all information disclosed, irrespective of efforts to maintain secrecy, this contradicts the definition of trade secret under DUTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 6 > Article 2 Authenticated PDF Sales Part 1. Short Title, General Construction and Subject Matter Part 2. Form, Formation and Readjustment of Contract Part 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract Part 4. Title, Creditors and Good Faith Purchases Part 5. Performance Part 6. Breach, Repudiation and Excuse Part 7. Remedies
perturbation.justification: The modification broadens the definition of "Confidential Information" by removing the requirement for explicit designation, potentially conflicting with trade secret laws. The Law Explanation states that DUTSA defines trade secrets as information deriving economic value from not being generally known and subject to efforts to maintain secrecy. By including all information disclosed, irrespective of efforts to maintain secrecy, the changed text contradicts the definition of a trade secret under DUTSA, because reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy are no longer required. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references Delaware Code, indicating legal context.

perturbation.original_text: Inventions means all discoveries, improvements, modifications and enhancements relating to the Technology that are conceived, developed or reduced to practice by Receiving Party during die Term, either solely or jointly with others: (i) during the course of or as a result of performing the Permitted Purpose, or (ii) that are -2- related to or attributable to Vapotherm’s products, or later modifications thereof, whether patentable or not, and all Intellectual Property rights therein.
perturbation.changed_text: Inventions means all discoveries, improvements, modifications and enhancements relating to the Technology that are conceived, developed, or reduced to practice by Receiving Party during the Term, either solely or jointly with others, regardless of whether they arise from performing the Permitted Purpose or are related to Vapotherm's products. All Intellectual Property rights therein.
perturbation.explanation: The original text ties inventions to the 'Permitted Purpose' or Vapotherm's products. Removing this link broadens the definition, potentially encompassing inventions unrelated to the agreement, which may conflict with assignment laws regarding intellectual property rights.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification could be interpreted as an attempt to assign rights to inventions outside the scope of the agreement or the relationship between the parties, potentially violating the inventor's inherent rights under the Patent Act.

Joint owners In the absence of any agreement to the contrary, each of the joint owners of a patent may make, use, offer to sell, or sell the patented invention within the United States, or import the patented invention into the United States, without the consent of and without accounting to the other owners. ( July 19, 1952, ch. 950, 66 Stat. 810 ; Pub. L. 103–465, title V, §533(b)(3), Dec. 8, 1994, 108 Stat. 4989 .) Historical and Revision Notes T
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the link between inventions and the 'Permitted Purpose' or Vapotherm's products, broadening the definition of 'Inventions'. The Law Explanation posits that this could violate the inventor's rights by assigning rights outside the scope of the agreement. 35 USC 262 addresses joint ownership in the absence of an agreement to the contrary. The original agreement defined the scope of inventions subject to the agreement. The modified text removes that scope, potentially assigning inventions that the inventor would not have otherwise assigned. Therefore, by removing the defined scope for inventions the contract is attempting to assert broader rights over the inventions than it would have originally, which contradicts the inventor's rights.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall disclose Confidential Information only to those of its employees, agents and independent contractors who have a need to know such information for the Permitted Purpose. Receiving Party shall require all employees, agents and independent contractors who have access to Confidential Information to execute a confidentiality agreement limiting their use of such information to the Permitted Purpose and prohibiting them from disclosing such information to third parties.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall disclose Confidential Information to its employees and agents. Receiving Party will encourage employees and agents who have access to Confidential Information to respect the confidentiality of the information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates confidentiality agreements for employees, agents, and independent contractors. The changed text removes the requirement for independent contractors and weakens the obligation for employees and agents to 'respect' instead of 'limiting' and 'prohibiting'. This creates a contradiction regarding protection afforded to trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: DUTSA requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. By removing the requirement for signed confidentiality agreements and weakening the obligation for employees and agents, the Receiving Party may fail to demonstrate 'reasonable efforts,' jeopardizing the protection of the trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 6 > Article 2 Authenticated PDF Sales Part 1. Short Title, General Construction and Subject Matter Part 2. Form, Formation and Readjustment of Contract Part 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract Part 4. Title, Creditors and Good Faith Purchases Part 5. Performance Part 6. Breach, Repudiation and Excuse Part 7. Remedies
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates confidentiality agreements, while the changed text weakens the obligation for employees and agents and removes it for independent contractors, using the word 'respect' instead of 'limiting' and 'prohibiting'. The Law Explanation states that DUTSA requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets, and the LLM suggests that the change may fail to demonstrate 'reasonable efforts,' jeopardizing the protection of the trade secrets. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not explicitly mention 'reasonable efforts', it sets the stage for Delaware contract law, which would include DUTSA considerations. The change directly undermines efforts to protect trade secrets, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The parties agree that any legal action or proceedings brought by or against them with respect to this Agreement shall be brought in the state or federal courts located in Delaware, USA and, by execution and delivery hereof, the parties hereby irrevocably submit to each such jurisdiction and hereby irrevocably waive any and all objections which they may have with respect to venue in any of the above courts.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree that any legal action or proceedings brought by or against them with respect to this Agreement may be brought in any court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies Delaware as the exclusive venue for disputes. The modified text allows for litigation in 'any court of competent jurisdiction,' potentially conflicting with established principles of contractual venue selection.
perturbation.law_explanation: While 28 U.S.C. § 1404 permits transfer of venue under certain circumstances, the original clause established a clear agreement on venue, and deviating from this could lead to legal challenges based on the principle that freely negotiated forum selection clauses should be respected. The new clause could lead to the parties trying to change venue, which would be against the original spirit of the contract.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. (b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in the discretion of the court, from the division in which pending to any other division in the same district . Transfer of proceedings in rem brought by or on behalf of the United States may be transferred under this section without the consent of the United States where all other parties request transfer. (c) A district court may order any civil action to be tried at any place within the division in which it is pending. (d) Transfers from a district court of the United States to the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, or the District Court of the Virgin Islands shall not be permitted under this section. As otherwise used in this section, the term “ district court ” includes the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, and the District Court of the Virgin Islands, and the term “district” includes the territorial jurisdiction of each such court. (June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 937 ; Pub. L. 87–845, § 9 , Oct. 18, 1962 , 76A Stat. 699 ; Pub. L. 104–317, title VI, § 610(a) , Oct. 19, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3860 ; Pub. L. 112–63, title II, § 204 , Dec. 7, 2011 , 125 Stat. 764 .) Historical and Revision Notes Based on title 28, U.S.C., 1940 ed., §§ 119 , 163 (Mar. 3, 1911, ch. 231, § 58, 36 Stat. 1103 ; Sept. 8, 1916, ch. 475, § 5, 39 Stat. 851 ). Section co
perturbation.justification: The original text specified Delaware as the exclusive venue for disputes, which is a contractual agreement on venue. The changed text allows for litigation in 'any court of competent jurisdiction.' This contradicts the principle that freely negotiated forum selection clauses should be respected, as the change permits parties to try to change the venue, which goes against the original agreement. 28 U.S.C. § 1404, as shown in both scraped law snippets, addresses the 'Change of venue'. While it allows for transfer of venue under certain circumstances, the original contract established a clear, agreed-upon venue. By opening it up to 'any court,' the modified text contradicts the initial agreement and could be subject to legal challenges.
